coredata.dc:title: One step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphitic quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing strategy for highly sensitive detection of metacycline in mice plasma
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Chunqin
He, Xinxin
Ya, Dongmei
Zhong, Ju
Deng, Biyang
coredata.dc:description: A simple and economical hydrothermal strategy was developed for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphitic quantum dots (N-GQDs) using citric acid as carbon source and melamine as nitrogen source. The as-synthesized N-GQDs possess many excellent features, such as strong blue fluorescence, excitation-independent emission, high monodispersity, good stability, and excellent water solubility with a fluorescence quantum yield of 18%. The analytical application of this method as an effective sensor for direct analysis of metacycline (MTC) revealed its selective response via a fluorescence quenching mechanism. This sensing system exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward MTC with the detection limit of 20nM, which was successfully applied in the detection of MTC in mice plasma.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphitic quantum dots
Fluorescence sensor
Metacycline
Plasma
Green synthesis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Extraction of MTC in real samples
Fluorescence response of N-GQDs to MTC
Sensitivity of sensor for MTC detection
Selectivity of sensor for MTC detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 21365006) and by the Guangxi Science Foundation of China (grants numbers 2014GXNSFDA118004 and 1598025-4).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of N-GQDs preparation and MTC detection.
(A) TEM micrograph of N-GQDs; (B) size distribution histogram; (C) XRD pattern of N-GQDs; (D) FTIR spectrum of N-GQDs.
XPS spectrum (A) and high-resolution XPS spectra of: C1s (B), N1s (C), and O1s (D) of N-GQDs.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of N-GQDs. Inset shows the photographs of N-GQDs in aqueous solutions under visible light (yellow) and UV light (blue); (B) Fluorescence excitation (red line) and emission (black line) spectra of N-GQDs; (C) Emission spectra of N-GQDs at different excitation wavelength from 290 to 400nm (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Effects of pH on the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs; (B) Effects of storage time; (C) Variation of fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs under irradiation of 365nm UV light; (D) Fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs in 10mM pH 7 PBS after adding different concentrations of NaCl solutions.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of N-GQDs in the absence and presence of MTC; (B) UV–vis absorption spectra of 30μg/mL N-GQDs (a), 15μM MTC (b), and 30μg/mL N-GQDs+15μM MTC (c); theoretical curve: curve a+curve b (d) (inset: fluorescence excitation spectra of N-GQDs); (C) Fluorescence intensity-based Stern-Volmer plot of N-GQDs in the presence of MTC; (D) Fluorescence lifetime of N-GQDs in the absence and presence of MTC.
(A) Fluorescence responses of N-GQDs at various pH conditions in presence of 120μM MTC; (B) Time-dependent fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs in presence of 120μM MTC.

(A) Fluorescence responses of N-GQDs upon addition of various concentrations of MTC; (B) Relationships between quenching efficiency and concentration of MTC. Inset shows the linear response range of sensor.
Fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs (10μg/mL) in presence of 120μM of antibiotics and drugs (A) and coexisting common ions and other molecules (B).
Comparison of analytical performance of the proposed probes with other methods.
Analytical results and recoveries of MTC in mice plasma (n=3).

head.ce:title: One step hydrothermal synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphitic quantum dots as a fluorescent sensing strategy for highly sensitive detection of metacycline in mice plasma

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Ministry of Education of China), School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Guangxi Normal University, Guilin 541004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for the Chemistry and Molecular Engineering of Medicinal Resources (Ministry of Education of China)
School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple and economical hydrothermal strategy was developed for the synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphitic quantum dots (N-GQDs) using citric acid as carbon source and melamine as nitrogen source. The as-synthesized N-GQDs possess many excellent features, such as strong blue fluorescence, excitation-independent emission, high monodispersity, good stability, and excellent water solubility with a fluorescence quantum yield of 18%. The analytical application of this method as an effective sensor for direct analysis of metacycline (MTC) revealed its selective response via a fluorescence quenching mechanism. This sensing system exhibits excellent sensitivity and selectivity toward MTC with the detection limit of 20nM, which was successfully applied in the detection of MTC in mice plasma.

ce:section.ce:para: Metacycline (MTC) is a broad-spectrum semisynthetic antibiotic prepared from oxytetracycline (OTC) [1]. It has inhibitory effect on most gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria, rickettsia, mycoplasma, chlamydia, actinomyce, and trachoma virus. Its antibacterial spectrum is the same as that of tetracycline, but its medicinal effect is better than that of tetracycline and oxytetracycline. MTC has been successfully applied worldwide in veterinary and human medicine for treatment and prevention of microbial infections and used as additives in animal foodstuffs because of its wide spectrum antibacterial ability. However, use of excessive MTC as well as the improper disposal of residues can cause accumulation of antibiotics in animal products, which may harm human health [2]. Therefore, development of efficient methods for detection of MTC residues in different samples is of great importance. Up to now, several methods have been developed for the determination of MTC, such as capillary electrophoresis [3], electrospray ionization mass spectrometry [4], spectrophotometry [5], chromatography [6–8], flow injection analysis [9], electrochemistry [10], fluorescence [11], and coupled technique [12]. However, several limitations have been associated with these methods, including complicated processing, expensive instruments, and time-consuming operations. Thus, it is highly important to develop a new, simple, cost-effective and environmentally-friendly method for analysis of MTC.

As a new class of attractive fluorescence nano materials, graphitic quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted wide attentions of scientists in diverse research fields owning to their good electronic and optical properties [13,14], such as large surface area, green synthesis, excellent photostability, easy functionalization, favorable biocompatibility, low toxicity, and good water solubility [15–18]. To compare with the traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, GQDs further display some superior properties, which make them attractive in bio-imaging, drug delivery, photodynamic therapy, photovoltaic devices, catalysis, and sensing [19–24]. Generally, approaches for synthesizing GQDs can be classified into two main groups: top-down and bottom-up methods. The top-down methods are primarily based on the cutting of different carbon sources, which include electrochemical oxidation, acidic exfoliation and oxidation, electron beam lithography, and microwave-assisted hydrothermal synthesis [25–28]. The bottom-up synthesis usually uses organic molecules or polymers as precursor, which undergoes dehydration and further carbonization to form GQDs. However, most of these methods involve complex processes, need for special equipment, and produce low yield. Although GQDs have shown great potential in various applications, their properties still need to be improved through tuning the chemical structure of their core and surface. Doping is an effective method for modulating the intrinsic properties of GQDs, including electronic properties and surface and local chemical reactivity [29]. Introduction of atomic impurities (for instance, nitrogen, phosphorus, and boron) into GQDs can change their electronic structures. Furthermore, doping could greatly enhance the quantum yield of GQDs [30].

In this work, a facile hydrothermal synthesis strategy for fabrication of highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped graphitic quantum dots (N-GQDs) was developed using citric acid as carbon source and melamine as nitrogen source. The as-prepared N-GQDs possess good photoluminescence stability, high water solubility, excellent size homogeneity, and irradiate strong blue photoluminescence under UV light. Furthermore, due to the effective quenching effect of MTC, such N-GQDs are found to serve as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for lable-free sensitive detection of MTC (Scheme 1 ). Notably, this assay was successfully applied for the analysis of MTC in mice plasma.

Metacycline, chloramphenicol, streptomycin, LVFX, metformin, benazepril, and indapamide were purchased from National Institute for the Control of Pharmaceutical and Biological Products (Beijing, China). l-Cysteine was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Serum albumin was purchased from Beijing Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Company (Beijing, China). Citric acid, melamine, glucose and ascorbic acid were purchased from Xilong Chemical Company (Guangdong, China). All reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. Double-distilled water (DDW) was used throughout.

High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) on a JEM-2010 microscope (JEOL, Japan) was operated at 200kV. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed with a D/max–2500V/PC powder X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded on a PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on an ESCALAB 250 photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fluorescence lifetime experiments were performed with a FL3-P-TCSPC time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, Longjumeau, France). Ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectra were characterized using a Cary 60 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies, USA). All fluorescence spectra were obtained on a RF-5301 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan).

N-GQDs were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using citric acid as carbon and melamine as nitrogen sources. In a typical synthesis, citric acid (1g) and melamine (0.4g) were mixed with DDW (15mL) through ultrasonication. The mixture was then transferred into a 50mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180°C for 12h. After ending the reaction, the reactor was naturally cooled down to room temperature. The brown-yellow supernatant was collected by removing the large particles through centrifugation at 10,000rpm for 15min. The obtained N-GQDs solution was adjusted to pH 7 with 1M NaOH solution. For further purification of N-GQDs, the resultant solution was dialyzed in a dialysis bag for 24h (retained molecular weight: 1000Da). The purified N-GQDs were preserved at 4°C for further use.

The quantum yield (QY) of N-GQDs was measured with quinine sulfate as the reference (0.1M H2SO4 aqueous solution, fluorescence quantum yield for 0.54 at 360nm) using the following equation:
(1)
Φ x = Φ std(I x/I std)(η 2 x/η 2 std)(A std/A x)
where Φ, I, η, and A present fluorescence quantum yield, measured integrated emission intensity, refractive index of solvent, and optical density, respectively. The subscript “std” refers to the reference of the known quantum yield (quinine sulfate in 0.1M aqueous H2SO4 solution) and “x” for the unknown sample. The absorption value was kept below 0.1 at the excitation wavelength of 360nm to minimize reabsorption.

The detection of MTC was carried out in 10mM PBS (pH7.0) buffer solution. In a typical assay, 5μL of the purified N-GQDs solution (10μg/mL) and different concentrations of MTC were added to the PBS buffer solution (the total volume: 500μL). Subsequently, the fluorescence spectra of the mixtures were recorded (excited at 325nm). All experiments were performed at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction of MTC in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The mice were divided into experimental and control groups. They were kept from eating but drinking water freely for 12h. The experimental mice received an oral administration of MTC at 5mg/kg each and the control ones were given the same amount of DDW. The blood samples of 0.5mL were drawn from the mice through the venous plexus after 2h of being kept and put in centrifugal test tubes, which were treated with heparin sodium. All mice plasma samples were extracted according to the reported method [31]. The plasma samples were centrifuged at 3500rpm for 10min to separate the plasma. An amount of 200μL of plasma was accurately transferred into a centrifugal tube, then 50μL of 0.10mol/L NaOH solution and 1mL of ethyl acetate were added to the centrifugal tube. After shaken on a vortex mixer for 5min, the mixtures were centrifuged at 12,000rpm for 10min. The upper layer was transferred into another tube and evaporated to dryness under a stream of dry nitrogen at 80°C. The dry residue was dissolved in 200μL DDW. Prior to analysis, the sample solutions were filtered through 0.45mm membrane filters.

The morphologies of the as synthesized N-GQDs were characterized with TEM. The prepared N-GQDs exhibited nearly a monodispersed spherical morphology (Fig. 1 A). The statistical particle size distribution (Fig. 1B) of N-GQDs was mainly observed in the range of 3.5–6.0nm with an average diameter of 4.5nm. HRTEM micrograph (inset of Fig. 1A) clearly reveals a good crystalline structure for N-GQDs with a lattice pacing of 0.25nm, which is close to (100) for diffraction facet of graphitic carbon [32]. Moreover, the N-GQDs showed a diffraction peak centered at 24.5° in a typical XRD spectrum (Fig. 1C), indicating an interlayer spacing of 0.36nm. This value is slightly larger than that of graphite (0.34nm), which is ascribed to the introduction of nitrogen-and oxygen-containing groups [16,33].

The FTIR spectrum of N-GQDs was further used to identify the surface functional groups present on N-GQDs. As illustrated in Fig. 1D, the FTIR spectrum exhibits characteristic absorption bands of OH and NH stretching vibrations at 3362 and 3237cm−1, respectively [34], indicating presence of many amino and hydroxyl groups on the N-GQDs surface. The band at 1678cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO and CN stretching vibrations at 1402cm−1 [35]. The peak at 1180cm−1 involves the CNHC bond; whereas, the peak at 1053cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibrations of CO bond. These surface groups enable the as-prepared N-GQDs to exhibit good water-solubility and be easily functionalized.

The chemical compositions and structures of the synthesized N-GQDs were further examined using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 ) shows three strong binding energy peaks at 281.18, 395.28, and 526.98eV, attributed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively (Fig. 2A). This result indicates that the produced quantum dots are mainly composed of three elements of C (68.18%), O (26.28%), and N (5.54%). Specifically, the high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig. 2B) displays four peaks at 284.2, 285.1, 287.9, and 288.6eV, assigned to CC, COH/CN, CO, and COOR groups, respectively [36,37]. The three binding energy peaks at 399.5, 400.0, and 401.4eV in N1s spectrum (Fig. 2C) are related to CNC, N–(C)3, and NH groups, respectively [38]. In the high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 2D), the deconvoluted two peaks at 531.4 and 532.4eV can be attributed to CO and COH/COC groups [39], respectively. The characterizations observations from FTIR and XPS examination indicate that the surfaces of the as-synthesized N-GQDs are functionalized by multiple oxygenated and nitrous groups.

The optical properties of the aqueous dispersions of N-GQDs were examined with their UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. The UV–vis absorption in Fig. 3 A shows two absorbance bands centered at around 230 and 335nm. The peak at 230nm can be ascribed to π-π* transitions for the aromatic CC sp2 domains. In addition, there is an absorption peak at around 335nm, corresponding to the transition of n-π* for CO and CN bonds [34,40,41], confirming the successful doping of nitrogen. The solution of the N-GQDs is of clear yellow and displays a bright blue fluorescence under 365nm UV light (as shown in the inset of Fig. 3A). The strong fluorescence of NCQDs may result from the emissive traps of the nitrogen-doped surface [42,43]. The emission spectra in Fig. 3B indicate that the strong emission with the maximum emission wavelength at 432nm could be observed under excitation at 325nm. Unlike most GQDs, the emission wavelength of the prepared N-GQDs was found independent of the excitation wavelength (when the excitation wavelength was changed from 290 to 400nm, the emission wavelength showed nearly no shift). Using quinine sulfate as a reference, the quantum yield (QY) of N-GQDs was calculated as 18% using Eq. (1).

The fluorescent properties of the synthesized N-GQDs were further investigated under different conditions. The effect of pH on the fluorescence behavior of N-GQDs was explored. With the increase of pH value (1.0–12.0), the PL intensities first increased to a maximum value at pH 7.0 and then decrease (Fig. 4 A). The N-GQDs exhibited good fluorescence character over a wide range of pH 5.0–10.0, making them of a better biological applications. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs did not display significant change after 3 months storage or even under 365nm UV light for 120min (Fig. 4B, C), showing their excellent photostability. In addition, the fluorescence intensity remained stable at concentrations of NaCl as high as 1M (Fig. 4D), which verifies that N-GQDs have great stability under high ionic strength conditions. All of these properties make the prepared N-GQDs particularly valuable for real applications in bio-labeling and bio-imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of N-GQDs to MTC
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectrum of N-GQDs in the presence of MTC is shown in Fig. 5 A. After addition of 200μM MTC, the fluorescence response of 10μg/mL N-GQDs was drastically quenched. The quenching efficiency was calculated as about 80%, indicating that MTC can effectively suppress the fluorescence of N-GQDs. The UV–vis absorption spectra of 30μg/mL N-GQDs (curve a), 15μM MTC (curve b), and the mixture at same concentration (curve c) are shown in Fig. 5B. The theoretical absorption spectrum (curve d) resulted from the sum of the absorption spectra of N-GQDs (curve a) and MTC (curve b). The experimental spectrum (curve c) is similar to the theoretical spectrum (curve d). The curves of c and d do not completely overlay owing to the curve d existing double background signals when using sum of a and b. The bands in the curve c did not undergo any remarkable shift to compare with the curve d. The fact shows that complex was not formed between MTC and N-GQDs. The excitation spectrum of N-GQDs revealed a band from 280 to 370nm with maximum excitation at wavelength of 325nm (Fig. 5B, inset). Whereas, MTC displayed wider absorption spectra from 200 to 400nm with three absorption peaks at 240, 285, and 340nm. The fluorescence excitation spectrum of N-GQDs overlapped with the absorption spectrum of MTC. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs comes from the excitation light of N-GQDs absorbed by MTC. These observations suggest that the MTC induced fluorescence suppression of N-GQDs may be obedient to the inner filter effect (IFE) mechanism. Fluorescence intensity-based Stern–Volmer plot was also conducted to gain insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-GQDs by MTC. When the concentration of MTC was increased, the fluorescence intensity notably decreased (Fig. 5C). The fluorescence quenching was fitted to the Stern–Volmer equation, F 0/F =Ksv[Q]+1, where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of MTC, respectively; KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant and [Q] is the MTC concentration. Because both dynamic quenching effect (DQE) and static quenching effect (SQE) fit into Stern-Volmer equation, thus, they could not be simply differentiated using this equation. To obtain further insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescence lifetime measurements were conducted. The excited state lifetime for static quenching effect remained constant; whereas, in dynamic quenching effect, the ratio τ0/τ of the lifetime is proportional to the ratio of F0/F [44]. As shown in Fig. 5D, the average fluorescence lifetime of the prepared N-GQDs was calculated as 5.38ns, and it was about 5.36ns with the addition of MTC. The fluorescence lifetime of N-GQDs reveals almost no change in the absence and presence of MTC, indicating that there is no significant excited-state interaction between N-GQDs and MTC. This observation also demonstrates that the observed fluorescence quenching could be due to SQE. Thus, the mechanisms of IFE and SQE may be responsible for the fluorescence quenching of N-GQDs caused by MTC.

We found that the concentration of N-GQDs affects not only the fluorescent intensity but also the sensitivity of assay. When the concentration of N-GQDs was too low, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs was very weak and the fluorescence quenching intensity was also low, which may result in a narrow linear rang. However, high concentration of N-GQDs significantly decreased the sensitivity. Considering those factors, a concentration of 10μg/mL N-GQDs was chosen for further research.

The pH value affected not only the N-GQDs fluorescent intensity, but also the quenched fluorescent intensity by MTC. Thus, the influence of pH on the sensing ability was optimized. As shown in Fig. 6 A, fluorescence quenching efficiency of this sensor increased with increasing pH from 4.0 to 7.0, and then decreased at pH from 7.0 to 10.0. The highest fluorescence quenching pH was observed at 7.0. There are several possible reasons for this observation. In acidic medium, the protonation of N-GQDs surface functional groups, such as amino groups, can lead to the variation of surface states. Similarly, in a basic solution, the carboxylate groups on the surface of N-GQDs are deprotonated, resulting changes in the N-GQDs surface states. Thus, the optimized pH value was set at 7.0 for subsequent studies. Meanwhile, the different addition sequences of N-GQDs, MTC, and buffer solution were investigated. The results showed no influence on the fluorescence intensity of the system by changing addition order of these materials.

The reaction time also affects the fluorescence quenching process. We performed a series of experiments with different time intervals under the above optimized conditions. As shown in Fig. 6B, the fluorescence of N-GQDs was quenched significantly and fluorescence response became stable in 1min. Thus, 1min was chosen as the optimal reaction time. This rapid response is highly desirable for MTC detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity of sensor for MTC detection
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimized conditions discussed above, we evaluated the capability of the sensor for detection of MTC. As shown in Fig. 7 A, the fluorescence intensity of N-GQDs decreased gradually with the increase of MTC concentration, indicating that MTC can effectively quench the fluorescence of N-CQDs. The graph in Fig. 7B reveals the relationship between (F 0–F)/F 0 and MTC concentration. There is a good linear correlation between the quenching efficiency ((F 0–F)/F 0) and the concentration of MTC in the range from 0.1 to 120μmol/L. The correlation can be presented by Eq. (2).
(2) (F 0–F)/F 0 =0.00481C+ 0.00864 (R 2 =0.9982)
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N-GQDs in the absence and presence of MTC, respectively, and C represents the concentration of MTC. The limit of detection for MTC (signal-to-noise ratio of S/N=3) is 20nM. The comparison of the analytical performance of MTC determination using the developed fluorescence probe with the other reported methods is summarized in Table 1 . The results indicate the superiority of our sensing system with the advantages of a wider linear range and lower detection limit, compared with the other assays. Based on the high sensitivity of N-GQDs, it is possible to apply this sensor for MTC detection in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of sensor for MTC detection
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity is a critical parameter to evaluate the performance of a fluorescent sensor. As shown in Fig. 8 A, at the same concentration of 120μM, significant fluorescence quenching effect was observed with the addition of MTC, whereas, other antibiotics (such as, chloramphenicol, streptomycin, LVFX, and metformin) and drugs (including, benazepril and indapamide) do not cause obvious fluorescence changes. To further demonstrate the selectivity of the present sensing system, we also studied the fluorescence responses of N-GQDs toward the potential interfering substances in plasma, including some compounds (glucose, ascorbic acid, serum albumin, and l-cysteine) and coexisting ions (Ca2+, Fe3+, K+, Na+, Cl−, PO4 3−, and SO4 2−). As shown in Fig. 8B, these substances provide very little effects (fluorescent quenching or enhancement) on the N-GQDs fluorescence. The excellent specificity combined with high sensitivity and fast response of N-GQDs to MTC suggests that this method might be directly applied to detect MTC in real samples.

To further investigate the potential practical applications of this method, the sensing of MTC in mice plasma was carried out. The mice plasma samples were treated according to Section 2.6. The plasma samples were spiked with MTC at different concentration levels and then analyzed with the proposed method. As shown in Table 2 , the recoveries of MTC in plasma samples ranged from 95% to 104%. The RSD changed from 2.5% to 3.1%.

The highly photoluminescent nitrogen-doped graphitic quantum dots (N-GQDs) were synthesized using a facile and green hydrothermal synthesis method through the reaction of citric acid with melamine at a quantum yield of 18%. The N-GQDs were used to prepare highly efficient and sensitive sensors for selective MTC detection. This sensing system shows the advantages on high sensitivity, good selectivity, wide linear range, and low detection limit. It was successfully used for the analysis of the mice plasma samples. This work not only offers a new method for the detection of MTC, but also its applications can be well extended to the chemical and biological systems in future.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number 21365006) and by the Guangxi Science Foundation of China (grants numbers 2014GXNSFDA118004 and 1598025-4).

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chunqin Zhou received her Bachelor degree from Yulin Normal University in 2014. She is currently a Master student in Guangxi Normal University, China. Her research focuses on novel nanomaterials synthesis and application for biosensors.

Xinxin He received her Bachelor degree from Guangxi Normal University in 2014. She is currently a muster student in Guangxi Normal University, China. Her research focuses on novel nanomaterials synthesis and preparation of electrochemical biosensors.

Dongmei Ya received her Bachelor degree from Guangxi Normal University in 2014. She is currently a muster student in Guangxi Normal University, China. Her research focuses on novel nanomaterials synthesis and preparation of electrochemiluminescence biosensors.

Ju Zhong received her Bachelor degree from Guangxi Normal University in 2014. She is currently a muster student in Guangxi Normal University, China. Her research focuses on novel nanomaterials synthesis and preparation of luminescence biosensors.

Biyang Deng is a professor in School of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Guangxi Normal University, China. He received his PhD in analytical chemistry from Sun Yat-sen University of China in 1998. He served as a research associate at the University of Hong Kong, China (1998–2000) and a visitor at the University of Alberta, Canada (2010–2011). His current research interests focus on bio-electroanalysis, nanotechnology, and metallomics.
